content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
December 9, 1997
Debbie Rodgers Teasley
City Councilmember
City ofMoorpark
484 East Los Angeles Avenue #116
Moorpark, California 93021
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 1-97-545
Dear Ms. Teasley:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’ Since your questions are hypothetical, we
are treating your request as one for informal assistance.
2
QUESTIONS
You have asked whether you have a potential conflict of interest under the following
scenarios:
1. You or another agent in the Moorparlc or Simi Valley office of Coldwell Banker Town
& Country become a listing or buyers’ agent when homes or commercial development that were
built under the specific plan are put on the resale market?
2. You or another agent in the Moorpark or Simi Valley office of Coidwell Banker Town
& Country become a listing agent for home owners who want to sell their existing homes in
order to purchase new homes that were built under the specific plan?
3. You or another agent in the Moorpark or Simi Valley office of Coldwell Banker of
Town & Country make a presentation to one or more of the merchant developers to become a
referring source for resale listings, as described in question number two?
Government Code sections 81000
- 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not confer the immunity provided by formal written advice. (Regulation
1 8329(c)(3).)
428 J Street, Suite 450, Sacramento, California 95814 (916) 322-5660


File No. 1-97-545
Page 2
4. A “New Home Sales Division” is created in the Moorpark or Simi Valley office of
Coidwell Banker Town & Country and you or another agent from that office compete with the
new sales division of other real estate firms to become the new sales agent for one or more of the
merchant builders?
5. You or another agent from the Moorpark or Simi Valley office of Coidwell Banker
Town & Country provide a merchant builder (or a potential merchant builder) with a market
analysis ofhomes or land values in Hidden Creek Ranch? No fees or other form of
compensation is charged for this common real estate practice.
6. You or another agent from the Moorpark or Simi Valley office of Coidwell Banker
Town & Country, list or sell any ofthe parcels owned by the seven separate owners (other than
the Messenger Development Company)?
CONCLUSION
1-6. Under each ofthe scenarios above, with exception of question number five, it
appears that you will have a conflict ofinterest in the city council decision to approve the
specific plan. The foreseeability element is met since each ofyour questions indicate an intent to
take advantage of the business opportunities that will arise as a result ofthe development. Given
the magnitude ofthe development project, it appears that the materiality element is also met.
FACTS
You are a member ofthe Moorpark City Council. You are also a licensed real estate
agent with Coldwell Banker Town & Country. You serve as district manager for two Coldwell
Banker offices, one in Moorpark and the other in the neighboring city of Sinii Valley. Neither
office currently has a new homes division.
In your capacity as a real estate agent, you earn a commission on a sale if you are the
listing or selling agent. In your capacity as district manager, you are compensated by a base
salary and a 3 percent override of gross company dollar. Your income is based on 10 percent
sales income, 90 percent management salary and override.
Coidwell Banker Town & Country’s office is one of 29 real estate offices that currently
have listings in Moorpark as shown by the Multiple Listing Service, and one of 86 real estate
offices that are currently listed in the Simi Valley/Moorpark Multiple Listing Service. The City
of Moorpark is 12.4 square miles in area and is currently developed with approximately 7,447
single family dwelling units, 1,164 multi-family dwelling units, and approximately 15 acres of
commercial space. The population is currently estimated at 28,377.
Hidden Creek Ranch is a 4,322 acre area that is presently unincorporated. A specific plan
for Hidden Creek is currently pending before the Moorpark City Council. If the city council
approved the specific plan, proceedings to annex Hidden Creek Ranch to the city would be


File No. 1-97-545
Page 3
initiated. As proposed, the specific plan would allow for the development of 3,221 dwelling
units and 21.5 acres of commercial space. It is proposed to be built in phases over a period of 15
years.
Approximately 93 percent ofthe land within Hidden Creek Ranch is owned by the
Messenger Development Company. The remaining 7 percent is in seven separate ownerships.
Neither you nor your spouse has any financial interest in Hidden Creek Ranch or the proposed
specific plan. Neither you nor your spouse has ever received income from the Messenger
Development Company or any of the other seven owners in a real estate sale or leasing
transaction.
If the specific plan is approved, it is expected that one or more merchant builders will buy
the land and develop it. You have no information as to who those merchant builders might be.
Neither you nor any other agent of Coldwell Banker Town & Country has been approached by
anyone to handle the sale or property within Hidden Creek Ranch, whether or not the specific
plan is approved, or to handle new home sales or first time commercial sales or leases, if the
specific plan is approved.
ANALYSIS
General Rule
The purpose behind the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act is to ensure that public
officials, whether elected or appointed, will perform their duties in an impartial manner, free
from bias caused by their own fmancial interests or the financial interests ofpersons who have
supported them. (Section 81001(b).) In furtherance of this goal, section 87100 ofthe Act
prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest. As a member ofthe Moorpark City Council, you are a “public official” as defined in the
Act. (Section 82048.)
Financial Interest
An official has a fmancial interest in a decision within the meaning of section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on the official or on:
“(a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct
or indirect investment worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or
more.
(b) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or
indirect interest worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.
(c) Any source of income ... aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, received by or


File No. 1-97-545
Page 4
promised to the official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.
(d) Any business entity in which the public official is a director,
officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management.
(e) Any donor of... a gift or gifts aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, received by, or
promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time
when the decision is made ..
. .“ (Section 87103.)
As an employee and district manager, you have an economic interest in Coidwell Banker
Town & Country pursuant to section 87103(d). In addition, you have an economic interest in
any person, including your employer, who has been a source of income to you of $250 or more
within the past 12 months. (Section 87103(c).) Regulation 18704.3 (copy enclosed) provides
special rules for determining who are sources of commission income earned in a given sales
transaction. The following are deemed to be sources of income to a real estate agent within the
meaning of section 87103(c):
“(A) The broker and brokerage business entity under whose
auspices the agent works;
(B) The person the agent represents in the transaction; and
(C) Any person who receives a finder’s or other referral fee for
referring a party to the transaction to the broker, or who makes a
referral pursuant to a contract with the broker. (Regulation
1 8704.3(c)(3).)
The full gross value of the commission income for a specific sale is attributed to each
source of commission income in that sale or transaction. (Regulation 18704.3(d).) Pursuant to
this regulation, you will have an economic interest in Coidwell Banker Town & Country; the
clients you currently represent or have represented in the preceding 12 months; and persons who
receive finder’s or referral fees for referring a party to the broker, or who make a referral
pursuant to a contract with the broker. Please note that sources of “promised” income are also
economic interests. Commission income is deemed promised when the sale is pending (i.e., the
sale is in escrow). However, having a listing on property does not constitute promised income.
(Clay Advice Letter, No. 1-96-335, Bergman Advice Letter, No. A-93-297.)
Accordingly, you may not make, participate in making, or use your official position to
iafluence a governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will affect a
source of income to you of $250 or more within 12 months before the decision.
The amount of the value of gifts is adjusted biennially. Currently, a donor of a gift or gifts aggregating
$290 or more is a potentially disqualifying fmancial interest.


File No. 1-97-545
Page 5
Foreseeability
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at the time a
governmental decision is made depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable ifthere is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. Certainty
is not required. However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.
(In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)
The general rule enunciated in Thorner is that where a business entity in which the
official has an economic interest makes a bid or a contract or is preparing to make a bid or a
contract, a financial effect on the business entity is reasonably foreseeable. Your questions
indicate that you and other agents of Coldwell Banker Town & County intend to take advantage
ofthe business opportunities that will arise as a result ofthe development at Hidden Creek Ranch
(e.g., becoming a listing or buyers’ agent when homes or commercial development built under
the specific plan are put on the resale market; becoming a listing agent for home owners who
want to sell their existing homes in order to purchase new homes built under the specific plan;
becoming a referring source for resale listings for one or more merchant builders; opening a New
Home Sales Division; selling parcels owned by the seven separate owners). Therefore, it is
reasonably foreseeable that approval of the specific plan will have a financial effect upon you
and Coldwell Banker Town & Country, a potentially disqualifying source of income to you. In
addition, if a former or current client ofyours, who is a disqualifying source of income as
discussed above, is planning to sell or buy a home or commercial property as a result of the
development at Hidden Creek Ranch, it is reasonably foreseeable that approval of the specific
plan will have a financial effect on your client, an additional potentially disqualifying source of
income to you.
Materiality
Once you have established that an effect is reasonably foreseeable, you must determine
whether that effect is material. The Commission has adopted a series ofregulations that provide
guidance concerning whether the foreseeable financial effect of a decision is material. These
regulations apply different standards depending on whether the decision will directly or
indirectly affect the official’s economic interest. A person or business entity is directly involved
in a decision before an official’s agency when that person or entity initiates, is the named party
in, or is the subject of the proceeding concerning the decision. (Regulation 18702.1(b).) Your
facts do not indicate that you have an economic interest directly involved in the decision.
Approval of the specific plan may have an indirect material effect on a current or former
client who plans to sell or purchase residential or commercial property as a result of the
development at Hidden Creek Ranch. For individuals, the appropriate standard to determine
materiality is contained in regulation 18702.6 (copy enclosed). The effect of a decision on an
individual who is a source of income will be material if:


File No. 1-97-545
Page 6
“(a) The decision will affect the individual’s income, investments,
or other tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other than real
property) by $1,000 or more; or
(b) The decision will affect the individual’s real property interest
in a manner that is considered material under Section 18702.3 or
Section 18702.4.” (Regulation 18702.6.)
Approval ofthe specific plan may have an indirect material effect on your employer,
Coidwell Banker Town & Country or on a client that is a business entity. For business entities,
the appropriate standard to determine materiality is contained in regulation 18702.2 (copy
enclosed). The standards provided in the regulation are based on the financial size ofthe
business entity in which the official has an economic interest. For relatively small businesses,
the effect a decision will be material if:
“(1) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
gross revenues for a fiscal year of $10,000 or more; or
(2) The decision will result in the business entity incurring or
avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing
expenses in the amount of $2,500 or more; or
(3) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
value of assets or liabilities of $10,000 or more.” (Regulation
18702.2(g).)
You must apply the standards set forth above to determine whether the decision to
approve the specific plan will have a material effect upon one ofyour financial interests. The
City ofMoorpark is 12.4 square miles in area and is currently developed with approximately
7,447 single family dwelling units, 1,164 multi-family dwelling units, and approximately 15
acres of commercial space. As proposed, the specific plan would allow for the development of
3,221 dwelling units and 21.5 acres of commercial space. Given the magnitude ofthe proposed
development, it appears likely that you, Coldwell Banker Town & Country and your clients who
plan to buy or sell as a result ofthe development will be materially affected by the city council
decision to approve the specific plan.
Finally, approval of the specific plan may have a material effect upon you. For public
officials, the appropriate standard to determine materiality is contained in regulation
18702.l(a)(4). The effect of a decision is material on a public official if:
“The decision will result in the personal expenses, income, assets
or liabilities of the official or his or her immediate family
increasing or decreasing by at least $250 in any 12-month
period ....“
(Regulation 18702.l(a)(4).)


File No. 1-97-545
Page 7
If you have ally other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By.
Julia Butcher
Graduate Legal Assistant, Legal Division

more.

(c) Any source of income ... aggregating two hundred fifty

& Country become a listing or buyers’ agent when homes or commercial development that were

Town & Country make a presentation to one or more of the merchant developers to become a
referring source for resale listings, as described in question number two?2 Informal assistance does not confer the immunity provided by formal written advice. (Regulation
1 8329(c)(3).)

4. A “New Home Sales Division” is created in the Moorpark or Simi Valley office of

new sales division of other real estate firms to become the new sales agent for one or more of the

CONCLUSION: 1-6. Under each ofthe scenarios above, with exception of question number five, it
